jokes.text: Bartender hands a drink to the wrong person — 'Happy birthday, Craig.' / 'Retirement. Thanks.'
jokes.setup: The girls have been making chaos at what appears to be a catering event; a person finally gets served
jokes.punchline: The drink was meant for someone else's birthday, but the recipient corrects it to 'Retirement' and accepts it anyway with flat gratitude — suggesting he's been waiting a very long time and doesn't care about the occasion mix-up
jokes.explanation: Extremely economic joke — two beats, zero waste. The deflation of 'Retirement. Thanks.' in a single deadpan line implies an entire sad story: a man who waited long for a drink, doesn't even bother correcting the occasion fully, just accepts it. Small but genuinely well-crafted.

jokes.text: 'Your ankle is the size of a grapefruit. How did you not realize it was sprained?'
jokes.setup: We cut from chaotic catering party footage to the morning after; Abbi is injured
jokes.punchline: 'By that point in the night, I wasn't feeling much of anything' — alcohol-induced obliviousness explained with complete nonchalance
jokes.explanation: Functional hangover comedy. The grapefruit simile is vivid. The explanation is honest and character-appropriate but the joke itself is fairly standard 'I was too drunk to notice my injury' territory. Gets the job done without distinction.

jokes.text: 'I learned that tequila is an upper...' / 'I feel like it was, like, psychological, like the Gazebo effect.' / 'Placebo effect?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is explaining why she powered through the catering gig despite the injury
jokes.punchline: Ilana confidently misnames the 'placebo effect' as the 'Gazebo effect,' delivered with complete conviction — Abbi gently corrects her
jokes.explanation: This is excellent character comedy. 'Gazebo effect' is a perfect Ilana malapropism — not random, not dumb, but the kind of confident near-miss that makes her distinctively Ilana. She's not stupid, she's just slightly off. The structure is perfect: the false wisdom delivered as confident insight, then Abbi's gentle one-word correction, then Ilana's breezy 'Yeah. Yeah.' accepting it without embarrassment. High quotability. Earns its 7.8 because it's deeply character-integrated — only Ilana says this, only Abbi catches it this way.

jokes.text: Lesley says 'girlfriend?' in alarm; Ilana immediately opines that 'Partner is more lesbian'
jokes.setup: Abbi calls Lesley her 'girlfriend' and catches herself; the label becomes the subject of awkward group debate
jokes.punchline: Ilana derails the potentially serious relationship conversation with her confident but incorrect lesbian label taxonomy, delivered while the couple is clearly trying to have a moment
jokes.explanation: Ilana barging into Abbi's romantic moment with confident misinformation is a reliable comedic engine for this show. The joke is character-integrated (Ilana can't help herself) but the observation itself isn't especially sharp. Works on the cringe/interruption level more than the wit level.

jokes.text: Lesley breaks up with Abbi mid-group hang, landing during the quibble over 'girlfriend' vs 'partner'
jokes.setup: The conversation is ostensibly about word choice ('girlfriend' vs 'partner'); the audience and Abbi expect a mild awkward moment
jokes.punchline: Lesley reveals she wants to end the relationship entirely — the petty vocabulary debate was concealing a much larger issue
jokes.explanation: The escalation from label-squabble to full breakup is a solid structural move. 'It's a lot bigger than that' delivers with the right weight. Not a laughs-out-loud moment but darkly funny in the Broad City cringe register — Abbi's confusion is palpable.

jokes.text: Ilana going through college options like a menu: 'Yale — Too Kavanaugh. Cornell? You wish. Dartmouth — Too goyish.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is about to open what appears to be an acceptance letter; she's riffing on Ivy League schools while building anticipation
jokes.punchline: Each school is dismissed with a hyper-specific, politically/culturally loaded one-liner rejection — 'Too Kavanaugh,' 'You wish,' 'Too goyish' — before landing on Hunter College
jokes.explanation: This is among the episode's best jokes. Three rapid-fire dismissals, each with a distinct comedic register: 'Too Kavanaugh' is sharp political commentary in two words; 'You wish' is self-aware about her actual academic standing; 'Too goyish' is deeply Ilana-specific Jewish cultural identity comedy. The structure is escalating and perfectly economical. Only Ilana Glazer's character in 2019 says this exact sequence. The cherry on top is landing at Hunter College — a public city school — with full triumphant energy. Earns the 8.3 because it's funny on multiple levels simultaneously and is completely character-specific.

jokes.text: 'Master's, bitch. Doctorate, bitch. MD, bitch.' / 'Well, Master's.' / 'Whoo! Grad student.'
jokes.setup: Ilana celebrates her Hunter College acceptance by rattling off increasingly impressive degree titles
jokes.punchline: She self-corrects back down to Master's after overshooting, then celebrates 'Grad student' with the same energy as if she'd said 'Doctor'
jokes.explanation: The escalation-then-deflation structure is executed perfectly. 'Master's, bitch. Doctorate, bitch. MD, bitch.' gets bigger and bigger — then the immediate self-correction 'Well, Master's.' followed by undiminished enthusiasm for 'Grad student!' is a small comedic gem. The joke is in her refusal to be embarrassed by the gap between her ambition and reality. Very Ilana. 'Bitch' as punctuation is a show signature and it lands every time here.

jokes.text: Store employee refuses student discount because the acceptance letter 'isn't a student ID'; Ilana argues she will be a student 'in like three months'
jokes.setup: Ilana, freshly accepted to grad school, tries to leverage her acceptance letter as a student ID for a discount
jokes.punchline: The employee's deadpan 'Oh, three months? That's almost as long as we've been having this conversation' is a perfectly calibrated burn
jokes.explanation: The employee's comeback is the real joke here — sharp, dry, and proportional. The absurdity of trying to use an acceptance letter as a current ID is very Ilana-brained, and the employee's refusal to play along provides perfect straight-man energy. 'That's almost as long as we've been having this conversation' is genuinely well-written and quotable.

jokes.text: 'Okay, Zahm Zena. Do we have a beef?'
jokes.setup: Ilana is getting pushed back by the store employee and escalates
jokes.punchline: She addresses the employee as 'Zahm Zena' — a made-up aggressive nickname that sounds like a WWE wrestling name — before the situation immediately pivots
jokes.explanation: The absurd nickname 'Zahm Zena' is a genuine Ilana-ism — confrontational but also somehow affectionate in its weirdness. 'Do we have a beef?' adds to the mock-tough-guy register. The joke is pure character; it doesn't have a traditional structure but it's funny because only Ilana says things like this.

jokes.text: The store employee who was 'a bitch the whole time' immediately pivots to admiring Ilana's curls and asking her to model
jokes.setup: Ilana accuses the employee of being rude and is about to storm out
jokes.punchline: The conflict instantly evaporates because a photographer spots Ilana's hair and everything flips — she goes from almost-fight to modeling offer in seconds
jokes.explanation: Good tonal whiplash — Ilana goes from 'Do we have a beef?' aggression to 'Moi? Merci beaucoup, monsieur' in a heartbeat, which is funny and character-true. The pivot is almost too fast, which is the joke. The absurd logic of 'you were rude but your hair is perfect so let's forget it' works.

jokes.text: Ilana's 'Moi? Merci beaucoup, monsieur' response to being complimented on her curls
jokes.setup: Photographer effusively compliments Ilana's hair
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately slips into mock-French affectation as if she's a grand dame being recognized — total character shift in one second
jokes.explanation: Pure Ilana character comedy in three words. The spontaneous switch to French affectation when complimented is perfectly in character — she performs for any audience, including a hostile one that just became an adoring one. Economy score is nearly perfect; it's four words doing enormous character work.

jokes.text: Ilana tells the person on the phone: 'Something seems very locked up inside of you, so when you decide to change your life with therapy, give me a call.'
jokes.setup: After the modeling offer, Ilana is excitedly talking to someone on the phone who isn't engaging; she can't get them to share her excitement
jokes.punchline: Rather than letting it go, she delivers an unprompted therapy referral as if she's a wellness professional — with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: Ilana projecting psychological depth onto a stranger who simply isn't excited about her modeling news is a perfect character bit. The unsolicited therapy recommendation delivered as gospel — 'when you decide to change your life' not 'if' — is confident and absurd. It's a Broad City specialty: Ilana treating her opinions as professional-grade truth.

jokes.text: Lesley's breakup speech: 'I'm a doctor. I do jiu-jitsu and have hobbies. And you... You just don't really feel like an adult yet.'
jokes.setup: Lesley is breaking up with Abbi and explaining why
jokes.punchline: Her credentials list ('I'm a doctor. I do jiu-jitsu and have hobbies.') before comparing them unfavorably to Abbi is both pompous and pathetic — the inclusion of 'have hobbies' as a professional flex is particularly deflating
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the self-satisfied pomposity of Lesley's list. 'I'm a doctor. I do jiu-jitsu and have hobbies.' — the word 'hobbies' landing as if it's the clincher is genuinely funny. It reveals Lesley's character: someone who has talked herself into believing she's exceptional while her criteria are embarrassingly basic. The cringe is earned because Lesley is convinced she's being profound.

jokes.text: 'Shenanigans?' / 'Your 20s are for wild shenanigans.'
jokes.setup: Lesley dismisses Abbi's life as 'a series of wild shenanigans'
jokes.punchline: Abbi repeats 'shenanigans' back with pure disbelief — the word choice is so condescending and old-fashioned that it becomes its own punchline
jokes.explanation: The word 'shenanigans' is funny because it's so hopelessly square — it's the word a disappointed dad uses, not a contemporary adult peer. Lesley using it to describe Abbi's life reveals more about Lesley's perspective than she intends. Also the title of the episode, which adds meta weight. The callback potential throughout the episode gives it rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: 'But aren't you 30?' — Abbi firing back at Lesley's 'grow up' speech
jokes.setup: Lesley lectures Abbi about needing to 'grow up a little bit' as if from a great height of maturity
jokes.punchline: Abbi's four-word deflation: if growing up is the standard, Lesley's not that far ahead — plus the implication that Lesley's rules are arbitrary
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy — four words dismantle Lesley's entire premise. The laugh comes from the gap between Lesley's confident superiority and the reality that she's barely older. The structure is textbook: let the target build up steam, then one precise deflation. Lesley's reaction ('Wow wow you're fucking nuts') being completely disproportionate to the mild observation makes it even better.

jokes.text: Abbi's defensive adult credentials: 'I write thank-you notes all the time. And my phone screen isn't even cracked. And guess what, Lesley? I'm going to a play today. I'm going to a matinée.'
jokes.setup: Lesley tells Abbi she needs to 'grow up'; Abbi defends herself
jokes.punchline: Abbi's evidence of adulthood is magnificently small — thank-you notes, an uncracked screen, and a matinée — delivered with total conviction as if she's presenting a résumé
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The list is escalating in the wrong direction — thank-you notes is the strongest point, an uncracked phone is already flimsier, and 'I'm going to a matinée' as the triumphant finale is perfect. Abbi is earnestly defending her adulthood with the most minor possible evidence, and the sincerity of her delivery is what sells it. The joke is character-specific: Abbi genuinely believes these things matter, which is both sympathetic and funny. 'I'm going to a matinée' in particular is the ideal capstone — it sounds mature but actually just means she's going somewhere in the afternoon.

jokes.text: Lesley notes the matinée tickets were 'probably paid for by Ilana's parents'
jokes.setup: Abbi presents going to a matinée as evidence of her adulthood
jokes.punchline: Lesley immediately undermines it: 'with Ilana's parents, who I'm assuming paid for the tickets' — destroying the adult credential in real time
jokes.explanation: Lesley's undercut is well-timed and lands cleanly. The observation that Abbi's 'adult' activity is funded by someone else's parents is a fair and funny point. Doesn't require much originality because it plays off the strong joke before it — it's a good second beat to joke #17.

jokes.text: 'Finally on the same page about something.' — Lesley's exit line after Abbi says they're done
jokes.setup: Abbi, indignant, finally tells Lesley they're done and kicks her out
jokes.punchline: Lesley's parting shot: 'Finally on the same page about something' — agreeing about the breakup while framing it as another instance of their fundamental incompatibility
jokes.explanation: Sharp exit line. Lesley's parting comment reframes the breakup as yet another data point proving they don't work — while also being a dig. The irony is layered: the one thing they agree on is that they're incompatible. Economy is excellent: eight words do a lot of work. The line is a little Lesley-specific — it has her clinical, slightly superior register.

jokes.text: Abbi throws Lesley's ugly hat at her; Lesley catches it and it looks immediately cute on her
jokes.setup: Abbi indignantly throws Lesley's 'ugly-ass hat' back as a final act of defiance
jokes.punchline: 'Of course it looks cute on you. Whatever. God!' — Abbi's pettiness is immediately undermined by the hat looking good on Lesley
jokes.explanation: The visual-plus-verbal structure works perfectly. Abbi's defiant gesture backfires immediately and she can't even sustain her anger — she has to acknowledge 'of course it looks cute on you.' The hat returning looking cute is both a cringe beat and a character moment: Abbi's powerlessness even in righteous anger.

jokes.text: Boot cracks phone screen; 'Oh, did the wig not protect it?' — Lesley's sardonic last shot on her way out
jokes.setup: Abbi tells Lesley her uncracked phone screen was evidence of her adulthood; Lesley's boot knocks the phone to the ground
jokes.punchline: Lesley's exit line — 'Oh, did the wig not protect it?' — is a callback to the cracked screen claim AND the detail that there's apparently a wig involved, all delivered with devastating calm
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best constructed jokes. It works on three levels: (1) it's a callback that destroys Abbi's proudest adult credential in real time, (2) the mention of 'the wig' implies an entire unseen absurd scenario from the night before, and (3) it's delivered as Lesley's final word with maximum casual cruelty. The structure is nearly perfect — setup was laid two full scenes ago and the payoff lands with force. 'Oh, did the wig not protect it?' is genuinely well-written: specific, unexpected, and devastating.

jokes.text: 'DevaCurl?! Oh, my freakin' God, Ilana!'
jokes.setup: Abbi learns that the modeling gig Ilana got scouted for is DevaCurl — a real hair care brand
jokes.punchline: Abbi's explosion of excitement — 'DevaCurl?!' delivered with the energy of someone hearing their friend is going to the Met Gala — is disproportionate and endearing
jokes.explanation: The joke is mostly in Abbi's performance — the brand name DevaCurl is funny partly because it's real and mundane, but the reaction treats it as a life-changing revelation. Works well as a friendship beat: Abbi is genuinely thrilled for Ilana over something relatively minor.

jokes.text: Ilana's DevaCurl celebration: 'Devacu-u-u-uuuuuuur...' (extended howl across a scene break)
jokes.setup: Ilana celebrates being scouted for DevaCurl
jokes.punchline: The celebration extends across a scene cut, with the howl bridging two different locations — the joke is in the absurd duration of the celebration and its transgression of scene boundaries
jokes.explanation: A pure editing joke — the howl crossing the scene transition is a well-executed visual/audio gag that requires the audience to notice the cut. It's playful formal experimentation that suits Broad City's style. The joke is in the construction more than the content, which gives it a nice rewatch quality.

jokes.text: Ilana dressed as a grad student: 'She got the plaid slacks. She got the pink sweater. She got the tortoise-shell clip, bish.'
jokes.setup: Abbi has dressed up as a grad student to post adult-looking Instagram photos after the breakup
jokes.punchline: Ilana announces the outfit elements like a hype person at a fashion show, treating plaid slacks and a tortoise-shell clip as major flex items
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in Ilana applying hype-person energy to extremely mundane garments. Plaid slacks do not get announced with 'bish.' The joke works because Ilana's enthusiasm is real and the subject matter is deeply unspectacular. Good character integration — this is how Ilana shows love.

jokes.text: 'I got this, like, AC drop in my eye before. It was just, like, air conditioning pre-cum... like Ninja Turtle juice.'
jokes.setup: Abbi notices Ilana's eye is red and irritated
jokes.punchline: Ilana's explanation — 'air conditioning pre-cum' followed by the clarifying simile 'like Ninja Turtle juice' — is a magnificent double-barrel of absurdist description
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's funniest line and one of the great Broad City absurdisms. 'Air conditioning pre-cum' alone is already an instant-classic Ilana phrase — unexpected, crude, but also kind of accurate (condensation drip from AC units is a gross NYC fact). Then she immediately follows with 'like Ninja Turtle juice' as a CLARIFICATION, which somehow makes it weirder and funnier. The second simile adding to rather than replacing the first is a sophisticated comedic move. It is perfectly character-integrated — only Ilana's brain produces this chain of association. Highly quotable. Earns the 8.5 craft score because it combines originality, absurdist specificity, and character voice at an exceptional level. The 'Ninja Turtle juice' detail is the kind of thing that sticks in your memory years later.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, I'll just flush it out when I cry at the play later.'
jokes.setup: Abbi questions whether Ilana's eye situation is okay
jokes.punchline: Ilana's plan to self-medicate with her own theater tears is delivered as practical wisdom
jokes.explanation: An excellent small joke. The logic is simultaneously absurd and weirdly sound — crying does technically flush your eyes. But using anticipated theater emotion as an eye-care strategy is a beautiful Ilana-ism: she's confident in her own unconventional solutions. 'When I cry at the play later' also implies she's certain she'll cry, which is charming. Very quotable, very character-specific.

jokes.text: Ilana's mother's ringtone: '♪ Lordy, lordy... 'Cause Janet be 40 ♪' followed by 'Yep, yep, yep, yep!'
jokes.setup: A call comes in from Ilana's mother
jokes.punchline: The ringtone is a custom song — 'Lordy, lordy, 'Cause Janet be 40' — with Ilana's accompanying 'Yep, yep, yep, yep! Whoo-whoo!' suggesting she set this up and loves it
jokes.explanation: A great small character detail — Ilana having a custom celebratory song for her mother's 40th birthday as a ringtone tells you everything about their relationship. The joke is the specificity of it: not just a ringtone, but a ringtone that references her mom's exact age. Ilana's enthusiastic accompaniment is the cherry on top.

jokes.text: Ilana's father introduces her as 'your sister is a DevaCurl hair model' before Ilana finishes walking in; Ilana's mother immediately redirects: 'please let us not forget this is Eliot's special day.'
jokes.setup: The family is gathering to celebrate Eliot's going-away; Ilana arrives with big news
jokes.punchline: Dad steals the focus for Ilana's modeling before she even arrives; Mom immediately course-corrects to remind everyone this is Eliot's day — classic family dynamics in two beats
jokes.explanation: Good family comedy: Dad as Ilana's hype-man immediately overshadowing the occasion, Mom as the voice of social correctness. It's warm and recognizable. The specific dynamic — Dad being more chaotic than the kids — is a nice inversion of sitcom norms.

jokes.text: Ilana's father tries to get a stranger to pay for a photo with Ilana: 'You want a picture with my daughter? She's a very famous hair model.'
jokes.setup: Dad is established as Ilana's biggest fan; the family is walking to the theater
jokes.punchline: Dad immediately monetizes his daughter's one-day modeling gig, trying to sell photo ops to a stranger on the street — 'very famous hair model' for a job she hasn't done yet
jokes.explanation: Dad calling Ilana 'very famous' before she's done the shoot is perfectly absurd, and trying to charge strangers for a photo with her is a great escalation of his character. 'She's a very famous hair model' said with complete conviction is a lovely comedic beat.

jokes.text: Dad: 'You got to cash in on this, El. One of those whats-their-faces is worth, like, a billion dollars.'
jokes.setup: Eliot objects to the public spectacle of Dad trying to sell photo ops
jokes.punchline: Dad's business logic — referring to hair model billionaires as 'whats-their-faces' — is hilariously vague and confidently wrong
jokes.explanation: Dad's entrepreneurial delusion is funny because of how he can't even name his reference points. 'One of those whats-their-faces is worth a billion dollars' is confident business advice based on absolutely no information. Consistent character work.

jokes.text: Mom: 'You know, you really should be putting weight on the good foot.' Abbi: 'Yeah, but it's... I wanted the heel, so it just, like, works.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is hobbling with a sprained ankle
jokes.punchline: Abbi has chosen fashion over medical advice — the heel makes the grad-student Instagram look work, so she's ignoring the injury
jokes.explanation: Abbi choosing aesthetics over medical advice is consistent with her character's performative-adulthood arc. The joke is small and warm — Mom's genuine concern is immediately dismissed because Instagram takes priority. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: 'Someone's making a dank Instagram story.' / 'Yes, it's a story about a grown woman at the theater. Theater, yes.'
jokes.setup: Abbi is photographing herself at the theater as part of her adult-Instagram revenge campaign
jokes.punchline: Her self-narration — 'it's a story about a grown woman at the theater' delivered with the solemnity of a nature documentary — is self-aware cringe comedy
jokes.explanation: The callback to her adult-Instagram arc is earned, and 'a grown woman at the theater. Theater, yes.' has a funny self-important quality — Abbi narrating her own Instagram story while being aware of how hollow it is. The double 'Theater, yes' lands as the kind of thing you say when you're trying to convince yourself.

jokes.text: 'Walk it off, Anne Frank!'
jokes.setup: The group is watching what appears to be an Anne Frank-themed play; a dramatic moment on stage where Anne Frank expresses frustration about the attic
jokes.punchline: Ilana, apparently absorbed in the play, shouts 'Walk it off, Anne Frank!' as if heckling an athlete — treating a Holocaust figure with sports-coach energy
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and one of the show's great lines. 'Walk it off, Anne Frank!' is extraordinary comedy. It works because: (1) it perfectly captures Ilana's complete inability to code-switch to social norms even in a theater setting; (2) the sports-heckle register applied to one of history's most tragic figures creates an enormous tonal collision; (3) it's four words with zero waste; (4) it's completely character-integrated — only a character like Ilana, with her specific mix of political awareness and social chaos, produces this; (5) it's simultaneously offensive and somehow not malicious — Ilana is genuinely encouraging Anne Frank. The joke earns its 9.0 craft because it's shocking, specific, character-perfect, economical, and genuinely original. It became one of the show's most quoted lines. The structure — we hear 'I'm so sick of this attic!' from the stage, then the heckle — is immaculate setup/punchline.

jokes.text: 'They're gonna win that Tony.' — delivered flatly after watching the Anne Frank play during intermission
jokes.setup: The family has just witnessed Ilana heckle an Anne Frank play
jokes.punchline: Dad's response to the whole experience is the confident, flat prediction 'They're gonna win that Tony' — completely ignoring the social catastrophe in favor of theater criticism
jokes.explanation: A beautiful tag to the Anne Frank joke. Dad's pivot from the heckling incident to theater-critic mode ('They're gonna win that Tony') is perfectly timed and reveals his character: unflappable, enthusiasm-first, oblivious to social disaster. The economy is excellent — five words deliver the joke. It's a strong second beat to an already excellent joke.

jokes.text: Abbi: 'Does your eye stink?' Ilana: 'Hoo! Yeah, it stinks.'
jokes.setup: Ilana's eye has been irritated; it now apparently smells
jokes.punchline: An infected eye producing an odor is disgusting in a very specific way — and Ilana's confirmation 'Hoo! Yeah, it stinks' includes what appears to be her smelling her own eye area
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of the eye storyline. The idea that an eye infection produces a smell is gross and funny, and Ilana confirming it with a 'Hoo!' — apparently reacting to her own eye odor — is a well-executed physical comedy beat implied through audio.

jokes.text: 'It smells like prosciutt.' — Abbi describing the eye smell
jokes.setup: Ilana's eye smells bad; Abbi is identifying the smell
jokes.punchline: 'Prosciutt' — dropping the final syllable of 'prosciutto' while also selecting prosciutto specifically as the comparison point for infected eye odor
jokes.explanation: Abbi casually identifying that Ilana's eye smells like cured pork is both disgusting and specific in exactly the right way. 'Prosciutt' with the casual dropped ending sounds like genuine NYC speech rhythm, not an affectation. The specificity of prosciutto — not just 'meat' — is what makes it funny.

jokes.text: Hospital conversation — the Glazers debate which hospitals have become condos or Whole Foods: 'St. Vincent's.' / 'It's condos.' / 'Cabrini!' / 'Also condos.' / 'That what's-his-name doctor on Houston St.' / 'It's not condos, but it's a Whole Foods.'
jokes.setup: The family needs to find a doctor or hospital for Ilana's eye emergency
jokes.punchline: Every New York hospital they suggest has been converted — either to condos or, brilliantly, to a Whole Foods — an escalating riff on NYC gentrification that ends with the unexpected variation
jokes.explanation: This is a genuine standout moment. The escalating riff on NYC hospital closures/conversions is sharp social satire disguised as comic desperation. 'Also condos' as the second beat is funny. But 'It's not condos, but it's a Whole Foods' — the variation that breaks the pattern while remaining equally damning — is the joke that elevates the sequence. It's not random; it's a real NYC fact (hospitals closing, then getting replaced by luxury amenities for the gentrifying class). The joke works as gentrification commentary AND as absurdist escalation simultaneously. The family casually rattling this off as shared knowledge is a great character detail about NYC natives. Earns the 8.0 because it's genuinely well-constructed and culturally specific.

jokes.text: 'Again, why do you love this city?' / 'You got to get an ambulance. You... You smell like a dog's ass.' / 'What'd you say?' / 'A dog's ass.' / 'Dog's ass... That's it?' / 'What?' / 'Mwah!'
jokes.setup: Father asks why Ilana loves a city where hospitals have become condos and Whole Foods
jokes.punchline: The sequence pivots to Dad describing Ilana's eye smell as 'a dog's ass' — Ilana doesn't object to the comparison but rather asks 'That's it?' — implying she expected worse — and then kisses him on hearing the full assessment
jokes.explanation: The inverted reaction — Ilana not upset by the dog's ass comparison, but almost relieved — is a great character beat. 'That's it?' implies she thought it might be worse, and 'Mwah!' as her response to his confirmation is weirdly touching in a Glazer family way. This is the Glazer family dynamic in miniature: deeply unconventional but genuinely loving.

jokes.text: Pedicab driven by a real estate broker: 'I'm not a pedicab driver. I'm a real estate broker. Read the sign.' / 'Then why do you have a pedicab?'
jokes.setup: Ilana hails what she thinks is a pedicab for her medical emergency
jokes.punchline: The pedicab is operated by a real estate broker who uses it to shuttle clients to apartments for 'multiple viewings' — an absurd but somehow plausible NYC real estate hustle
jokes.explanation: The concept of a real estate broker operating a pedicab for client transport is a genuinely original absurdist NYC premise. 'Read the sign' is the kind of detail that makes it feel like a real character, not just a plot device. The explanation — 'fastest way to get clients to apartments for multiple viewing' — is the kind of thing that makes insane sense in a New York context.

jokes.text: Pam reveals she hooks up her NYPD ex with 'a hand job once and a while' to get access to crime scene apartments
jokes.setup: Pam explains how she gets access to crime scene apartments for her real estate business
jokes.punchline: Her arrangement — occasional hand jobs for a former boyfriend in exchange for crime scene property leads — is delivered as standard business networking
jokes.explanation: Pam's total nonchalance about her crime-scene/hand-job business arrangement is great character comedy. 'Crime scene homes are my speciality' combined with the transactional sex-for-leads model is dark but delivered so matter-of-factly that it lands as comedy rather than grimness. This is a recurring Broad City type: the minor character with an entire wild backstory revealed in thirty seconds.

jokes.text: 'Wait a minute. Flex that thigh. Whoopsie! I can see your short and curlies. Let's go!'
jokes.setup: Pam is being persuaded to give Ilana a ride; she expresses exhaustion and shows her feet
jokes.punchline: Ilana spots Pam's exposed pubic hair when she flexes, declares 'short and curlies' with delight, and uses this as the final motivation to get Pam moving
jokes.explanation: Classic Broad City body-comedy that's cheerful rather than gross. 'Short and curlies' is a period-appropriate Pam detail, and Ilana's delight rather than embarrassment at the discovery is character-perfect. The joke pivots naturally into the chase sequence.

jokes.text: Ilana spots Lesley from the pedicab and says: 'Well, well, well, Lesley. One of these babies could've been yours.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is speeding through the city on Pam's pedicab for a medical emergency; she happens to spot Lesley
jokes.punchline: She takes a moment from the medical emergency to deliver a villain monologue to her friend's ex, implying Lesley missed out on something grand (the pedicab/chaos), then immediately refocuses
jokes.explanation: Ilana's 'One of these babies could've been yours' delivered from a speeding pedicab in the middle of a medical emergency is perfectly absurd. It captures her unhinged loyalty to Abbi — she can't miss an opportunity to stick it to Lesley even mid-crisis. The villain-monologue cadence applied to a pedicab moment is a great tonal joke.

jokes.text: 'Shit. Lighting is terrible in here. Gotta find another light... source.' (cuts to Ilana hanging out a window for natural light to take Instagram photos)
jokes.setup: Abbi is continuing her adult-Instagram revenge campaign from the theater bathroom
jokes.punchline: She hangs herself out of what appears to be a window or external building surface seeking better lighting — escalating the Instagram mission to physical danger
jokes.explanation: The Instagram campaign arc reaches its logical extreme: Abbi hanging out of a building for better lighting. The journey from 'Hence The New Yorker' to physical peril is a great character escalation. 'Gotta find another light source' as practical problem-solving before the visual reveal of the window situation is good structure.

jokes.text: The broker compliments Ilana's ass from behind the pedicab: 'You should see your ass from back here. It's amazing. It looks like a big heart.'
jokes.setup: Ilana is seated in the pedicab being ridden through NYC at speed
jokes.punchline: The broker's unprompted aesthetic appraisal of Ilana's anatomy — 'a big heart' — as casual NYC pedicab chatter
jokes.explanation: Mildly charming. 'Looks like a big heart' is a sweet (and also slightly absurd) way to phrase it. The scene has an odd warmth. Not exceptional but pleasant.

jokes.text: Abbi sees herself from outside: 'Shenanigans.' (flat, solo)
jokes.setup: Abbi is hanging out of a building, alone, mid-breakup, trying to get good Instagram lighting
jokes.punchline: Looking at herself objectively, she delivers the episode's title word that Lesley used — 'Shenanigans.' — accepting the diagnosis in a single word
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's structurally finest joke and the most emotionally resonant. One word — 'Shenanigans.' — spoken alone, flatly, while hanging out of a building mid-breakup-Instagram-revenge-campaign. It's simultaneously: (1) a callback that closes the Lesley arc; (2) a character moment of perfect self-awareness; (3) absurdly funny because the word choice is still Lesley's fussy word, not Abbi's; (4) a beat of acceptance that's both funny and unexpectedly touching. The economy is perfect — nothing else could go here, and nothing more is needed. It's the kind of joke that earns its 9.0 because it's doing comedy, character, and theme simultaneously in a single word. The structure over the full episode makes this land: we've been with Abbi's arc long enough that this one-word callback hits with accumulated weight.

jokes.text: Abbi, hanging between two buildings above concrete, picks up a call from Lesley and says 'Hey. Lesley.' / 'What's up... girl?'
jokes.setup: Abbi is physically stuck between two buildings; Lesley calls at this exact moment
jokes.punchline: Abbi's attempt to sound casual and unbothered — 'What's up... girl?' — while literally in physical danger is pure cringe comedy
jokes.explanation: Abbi forcing casual energy while in physical peril is a classic comedic situation — the desire to seem unbothered overriding self-preservation. 'What's up... girl?' with the pause before 'girl' is the detail that sells it: she almost can't get the studied-casual register right because she's terrified. Great character moment.

jokes.text: 'Okay, I'm sorry. I have to go before you. I'm a model.' (to the waiting room at the vet/doctor's office)
jokes.setup: Ilana arrives at a medical facility and there are patients waiting
jokes.punchline: She invokes her modeling status as a medical-triage credential, cutting the line because she's a model
jokes.explanation: Ilana treating 'I'm a model' as a legitimate emergency credential is quintessential character comedy. The self-importance is genuine in her mind — she has a shoot, this is serious, her face is her instrument. The casual delivery to a waiting room full of actual sick patients is wonderfully oblivious.

jokes.text: The doctor turns out to be a vet: 'Ilana, I told you I cannot spay-neuter a human, and it is not reversible.'
jokes.setup: Ilana bursts in to see a doctor; the doctor knows her and is already exasperated
jokes.punchline: The doctor is a vet, and this is apparently not Ilana's first visit — the pre-emptive 'I cannot spay-neuter a human' means she's asked before
jokes.explanation: Outstanding joke. The reveal that the 'doctor' is a vet is already good. But the specific detail — 'I cannot spay-neuter a human, and it is not reversible' — delivered pre-emptively, means Ilana has asked this before, possibly multiple times. The vet has had to establish this boundary. The irreversibility caveat ('and it is not reversible') is a particularly sharp detail, implying Ilana may have tried to negotiate around that point. This is the kind of joke where the backstory revealed in one line is funnier than the line itself. The vet knowing Ilana well enough to lead with this is perfect. High quotability and rewatch value because it rewards you for imagining all the prior visits.

jokes.text: 'No, I-I Googled that... you were right about it.' — Ilana confirming spay-neuter research
jokes.setup: The vet reiterates she can't spay-neuter Ilana
jokes.punchline: Ilana confirms she Googled it and the vet is indeed correct — meaning she went home, researched human spay-neutering, verified it was irreversible, and apparently came back anyway about a different issue
jokes.explanation: The confirmation that Ilana researched this and verified the vet's medical assessment is a great button on the previous joke. 'You were right about it' implies she found this out empirically, not just took the vet's word. It expands the universe of Ilana's prior requests to this vet while closing the bit with perfect economy.

jokes.text: Vet: 'Ugh. Okay.' / 'That looks bad. Have a seat.' / 'Okay, good girl. Good girl. Good... Good girl!'
jokes.setup: The vet looks at Ilana's eye and directs her to sit down
jokes.punchline: The vet unconsciously uses 'Good girl!' to Ilana — the same praise she'd give a dog patient — and keeps going, apparently unable to stop herself
jokes.explanation: The vet's professional reflex — praising a human patient like a dog — is a reliable comedic concept, but the execution is good because of the repetition. She says 'Good girl!' three times, escalating in absurdity. The structure implies she catches herself each time but can't stop. The three-peat is better than a single instance would be.

jokes.text: Abbi, suspended between two buildings, asks Lesley to call 9-1-1: 'But, uh, I do need... I do need you to call 9-1-1.' Then immediately: 'You pick dinner. It's totally on you. I'm cool with whatever.'
jokes.setup: Lesley has just re-broken up with Abbi who is suspended between buildings
jokes.punchline: Abbi pivots from 'please call 911' to casually negotiating dinner plans — simultaneous crisis management and relationship maintenance
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of medical emergency and dinner-date logistics in the same breath is very funny and very Abbi. Her desire to smooth over the breakup by being easygoing about dinner even while physically imperiled is both absurd and a recognizable relationship behavior. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: 'Jesus Christ, Abbi.' — Lesley's response to learning Abbi needs 9-1-1
jokes.setup: Abbi asks Lesley to call 9-1-1 after their reconciliation
jokes.punchline: Lesley's 'Jesus Christ, Abbi' — the reaction of someone who has just re-entered a relationship and immediately encountered its chaos — is the perfect response, delivered with the weariness of someone who knew this would happen
jokes.explanation: Three words doing substantial work. 'Jesus Christ, Abbi' acknowledges the 'shenanigans' thesis, affirms Lesley's concerns, and yet — she's still there, calling 9-1-1. The reaction beat implies she's both exasperated and somewhat resigned to accepting this is what dating Abbi means.

jokes.text: 'You know, I've gotten New York City water droplets in my mouth before and I was fine. I did have bloody stool for a week or two, but that's pretty typical for me in the summer.'
jokes.setup: The vet/doctor gives Abbi health advice about Ilana's eye
jokes.punchline: Abbi casually mentions 'bloody stool for a week or two' as if it's a seasonal New York City experience — 'pretty typical for me in the summer' — delivered without alarm
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the escalating casualness. NYC water droplets causing bloody stool is gross; 'a week or two' is alarming; 'pretty typical for me in the summer' delivered as cheerful normalization is the punchline. The entire sentence is a dark comedy of urban living — New Yorkers accepting genuinely awful things as background conditions. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: Vet diagnosis: 'This is conjunctivitis.' — Ilana: 'No!'
jokes.setup: Ilana has been insisting her eye is fine; the vet examines it
jokes.punchline: The vet delivers the mundane diagnosis (pink eye) and Ilana reacts with existential horror — 'No!' — as if told she has days to live
jokes.explanation: The proportionality joke: a common mild condition treated like a terminal diagnosis. Works because of Ilana's modeling stakes — this is actually a problem for her. The 'No!' is performed like a death sentence, which is funny given the context.

jokes.text: 'What if I'm paired with a curly-haired, non-binary model, and it's a love scene?'
jokes.setup: Vet says Ilana can't get close to anyone due to the conjunctivitis
jokes.punchline: Ilana immediately imagines the most specific nightmare scenario — not just that she can't model, but the exact worst-case pairing and situation that would be ruined
jokes.explanation: The specificity of Ilana's fear is the joke: not just 'what if I have to interact with someone' but the precise scenario — curly-haired, non-binary, love scene. The detail of 'curly-haired' (relevant to the DevaCurl shoot) and 'non-binary' (maximally Ilana's type) makes this very character-specific. It's her ideal shoot partner, now ruined by pink eye.

jokes.text: Vet gives Ilana a shot: 'And a little bit of a pinch.' / 'Mm!' — followed by 'So this is gonna clear up my eye and make me gorgus for the evening.'
jokes.setup: Ilana asks about treatment options; the vet prepares a shot
jokes.punchline: 'That's your third round of Hep B shots. You have another one scheduled for next month.' — Ilana accepted a random injection thinking it was eye treatment; it was a Hep B vaccine she's been getting on schedule
jokes.explanation: Strong misdirection: Ilana assumes the shot is for her eye, the vet reveals it was her Hep B schedule. The background detail — she's been getting these shots at the vet; she has a third one next month — implies this is not unusual for Ilana's life. 'Gorgus' (her slightly mangled pronunciation of gorgeous) is also a small character touch. The joke rewards the listener who catches the vet casually maintaining Ilana's vaccination schedule.

jokes.text: 'Two fuggin' weeks?! No, no, no! Look, honey, I'm a model now, and this here is my money maker.'
jokes.setup: Vet says the eye drops will clear up her conjunctivitis in about two weeks
jokes.punchline: 'This here is my money maker' — Ilana gesturing at her infected, smelly eye as a prized professional asset — delivered with the authority of a veteran model
jokes.explanation: Ilana referring to her infected, malodorous eye as 'my money maker' with veteran-model gravitas is a great character beat. She's been a model for approximately four hours and has fully adopted the professional persona. 'This here is my money maker' applied to a diseased eye is an excellent juxtaposition.

jokes.text: 'Don't you have something quicker, like... bovine growth hormone that Madonna shoves up her crack?'
jokes.setup: Ilana demands a faster treatment option than the prescription
jokes.punchline: She suggests bovine growth hormone (used on cattle) administered via 'Madonna shoves up her crack' as if this is a known celebrity beauty protocol
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best lines. 'Bovine growth hormone that Madonna shoves up her crack' is extraordinary — it combines: (1) real celebrity obsession with extreme beauty treatments; (2) bovine growth hormone as a specific and absurd escalation; (3) the administration method ('up her crack') as delivered completely matter-of-factly; (4) the implicit authority Ilana grants to Madonna's beauty regimen. The joke implies Ilana has done extensive research into celebrity anti-aging protocols and this is just a thing she knows. It's absurd but internally consistent with Ilana's character: she believes deeply in unconventional wellness and celebrity wisdom. Very high quotability — this is the kind of line that gets repeated. Earns the 8.3 because the specificity (bovine, not just 'some hormone'; Madonna specifically; the anatomical detail) elevates it well above a generic celebrity joke.

jokes.text: DevaCurl shoot begins with Ilana wearing an eye patch; director reacts: 'What's with the patch? What is this?' / Ilana: 'Today I'm serving... Kill Bill realness.' / Director: 'Yeah, more like Bob Costas realness.'
jokes.setup: Ilana arrives to the modeling shoot wearing an eye patch to hide her pink eye
jokes.punchline: She frames it as fashion ('Kill Bill realness'); the director immediately reframes it as a Bob Costas reference — the 2014 Olympics when Costas had an infamous eye infection on air
jokes.explanation: Sharp joke. 'Kill Bill realness' as spin for a medical eye patch is good; 'Bob Costas realness' as the deflation is excellent. The Bob Costas eye infection reference is a specific, datable pop-culture callback that perfectly matches the episode's recording period and is genuinely unexpected. The word 'realness' in both lines (borrowed from drag/ball culture) adds a layer of comedic register-mismatch. The specificity of Bob Costas — not just 'someone with pink eye' — earns the 8.0.

jokes.text: 'The rare triple helix... the Holy Grail of curls.' / 'You must sleep sitting up.' / 'Of course I do, I'm not an animal.'
jokes.setup: Ilana's mother is scouted to replace Ilana in the shoot; the director examines her curls
jokes.punchline: The director identifies her curl type as a 'rare triple helix — the Holy Grail of curls'; asks if she sleeps sitting up (presumably to maintain curl structure); the mother confirms 'Of course I do, I'm not an animal' — as if sleeping upright for hair maintenance is the minimum standard of civilization
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best exchanges. The 'triple helix' as premium curl nomenclature is a great comedic invention. But 'you must sleep sitting up' as a necessary implication — and the mother's complete, indignant confirmation 'Of course I do, I'm not an animal' — is what makes it exceptional. The mother's framing of sitting up to sleep as a basic marker of civilization (contrast: 'animal') is perfect absurdist character comedy. It establishes her as Ilana's mother immediately — the same confident absurdist conviction about her own choices. Very high quotability. Earns the 8.3 because the three-line exchange builds perfectly and the final line is a genuinely original comedic formulation.

jokes.text: 'Slay Curl Kween!' — Ilana cheering her mother's modeling debut
jokes.setup: Ilana's mother is taking over the modeling shoot
jokes.punchline: Ilana cheers her mother with 'Slay Curl Kween!' — maximum hype energy for a 40-something Jewish-Canadian mom at a hair shoot
jokes.explanation: Warm family moment filtered through Broad City energy. 'Slay Curl Kween' is very Ilana and the application of full hype-person vocabulary to one's own mother is charming. Functional rather than exceptional — the language does the work more than any structural ingenuity.

jokes.text: 'For the family honor.' (said twice — first when Ilana goes to the vet, now as a beat after her mother takes the shoot)
jokes.setup: Ilana has been invoking 'for the family honor' throughout the episode's medical/modeling crisis
jokes.punchline: The phrase returns here as Ilana's mother literally saves the family's modeling honor — the absurd phrase now has literal meaning
jokes.explanation: A good running gag with genuine payoff. 'For the family honor' was absurd when first used (about a hair modeling gig); it remains absurd but now accidentally accurate because the mother has literally saved the family's DevaCurl contract. The callback has weight because it's earned — Mom actually came through.

jokes.text: Ilana, after being replaced by her mother at the shoot, says quietly: 'For the family honor.'
jokes.setup: Ilana has just been replaced at her own modeling debut by her mother
jokes.punchline: 'For the family honor' — invoking a feudal sense of duty to explain why she is graciously accepting being bumped from her own shoot
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional economy. Four words that encompass the entire Ilana-Bobbi relationship dynamic — Ilana's absolute devotion to her family, the slightly deranged seriousness with which she takes mundane things, and the warmth beneath the absurdism. It's a tiny line doing a lot of work. High economy and character integration are justified.

jokes.text: 'The cast is electric'? What an idiot.' — one character reviewing the Anne Frank play program
jokes.setup: During intermission, someone reads the Anne Frank play's program
jokes.punchline: Dad reading 'The cast is electric' as a critic quote and dismissing the critic as 'an idiot' — presumably because he found the performance inferior — is confident criticism from a man whose daughter just heckled it
jokes.explanation: Dad's dismissal of the play's positive review — while his daughter literally heckled it — is a nice character beat. The irony of a man whose family disrupted the performance becoming a theater critic is good, and 'What an idiot' delivered flatly is his character in miniature.

jokes.text: Abbi recap: 'This psycho dumped you a second time in one day while you're hanging between two buildings, two stories above concrete. This bitch is a menace.'
jokes.setup: Abbi explains that Lesley re-dumped her during the window rescue
jokes.punchline: Ilana's flat summary — articulating the absurdity of the situation clearly — adds 'This bitch is a menace' as the legal verdict
jokes.explanation: Ilana's case-summary delivery is great — 'dumped you a second time in one day while you're hanging between two buildings' as if she's a prosecutor is genuinely funny. 'This bitch is a menace' as the closing argument is perfect. The recap structure works as comedy because the actual events have become so escalated that a flat summary is funnier than any commentary.

jokes.text: 'But then there was, like, this fire woman that was rescuing me. She was, like, carrying me down, and I was, like — Who are you? Like, she was hot.' / 'It's cool that I can see that now.'
jokes.setup: Abbi recounts her building rescue
jokes.punchline: Even mid-rescue, Abbi was attracted to her rescuer; she then frames being able to find women hot as a development she's now comfortable with — delivered as a small triumphant personal growth note
jokes.explanation: Abbi's bisexual self-recognition framed as casual evolutionary progress — 'it's cool that I can see that now' — is warm and character-true. The joke is gentle but well-executed: the attraction to a firefighter mid-rescue is very Abbi, and her noting it as growth is a quiet character moment with comedic timing. Connects to the season's Abbi arc.

jokes.text: 'Made in Her image.' / 'It's a Catholic thing.'
jokes.setup: Ilana says 'the female form — God's hottest creation'
jokes.punchline: Abbi says 'Made in Her image' (using female pronoun for God) and explains 'It's a Catholic thing' — which is not accurate Catholic theology
jokes.explanation: Abbi confidently misattributing feminist theology to Catholicism mirrors the 'Gazebo effect' energy — she's not wrong about the sentiment but wrong about the source, and delivers it with complete confidence. A small but genuine laugh, character-specific.

jokes.text: 'Covergirl! Slay, Mama!' escalating chant — Abbi and Ilana chanting 'Covergirl!' repeatedly for Ilana's mom's DevaCurl photos, until someone gets hurt
jokes.setup: They're looking at photos of Ilana's mother from the DevaCurl shoot
jokes.punchline: The chant escalates to 'Covergirl!' repeated with increasing intensity until physical contact causes injury — 'Ow! Dude! Ooh. Sorry. I'm sorry. Sorry.'
jokes.explanation: A warm friendship closing beat where their mutual enthusiasm becomes slapstick. The escalation of the chant to physical injury is a reliable Broad City move — enthusiasm taken past its natural conclusion. Functional as an ending beat; not especially original but emotionally satisfying.
